attributes.stem:
A 30-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with a four-hour history of right eye pain, worst on eye movement. She reports blurred vision with flickering in her peripheral visual field. She has never experienced these symptoms before. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. Two days ago, she experienced transient weakness in both legs lasting several hours after a hot shower. She denies headache, neck pain, speech disturbance or changes to bladder or bowel function.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Neurological examination of the lower limbs is normal. Extra-ocular movements are full but painful. There is no nystagmus or diplopia. Fundoscopy reveals right optic disc swelling.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with typical features of optic neuritis, which is commonly associated with multiple sclerosis (MS). The key features supporting this diagnosis are eye pain worsened by movement, visual disturbance, and transient neurological symptoms precipitated by heat (Uhthoff's phenomenon). The presence of optic disc swelling on fundoscopy confirms optic neuritis.

Acute disseminated encephalomyelitis typically occurs following an infection or vaccination and presents more acutely with multifocal neurological deficits, encephalopathy, and constitutional symptoms. It is much less common than MS.

Vasculitis affecting the central nervous system usually presents with other systemic features such as rash, arthritis, fever, weight loss, or renal involvement, none of which are present in this case.

Progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy is an opportunistic infection caused by JC virus reactivation in immunocompromised patients. It typically presents with progressive cognitive decline, visual deficits, and motor weakness.

Subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord, caused by vitamin B12 deficiency, characteristically presents with symmetric loss of posterior column function (vibration and proprioception) and does not cause optic neuritis.

The diagnosis of MS requires evidence of central nervous system lesions disseminated in both space and time, typically demonstrated on MRI. Many patients who present with isolated optic neuritis will subsequently develop MS, making this an important initial presentation to recognise.
learningPoints: Optic neuritis presenting with eye pain on movement and visual disturbance is a common initial presentation of multiple sclerosis.
Uhthoff's phenomenon, where MS symptoms worsen with increased body temperature, is a characteristic feature of multiple sclerosis.
Multiple sclerosis diagnosis requires evidence of lesions disseminated in both space and time.
references: Gelfand JM. Multiple sclerosis: diagnosis, differential diagnosis, and clinical presentation. Handb Clin Neurol. 2014;122:269-90. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24507522/
